name: Camino del Cid: Álvar Fáñez Branch Trail
name_original: Trijueque desde Ledanca — circular por la Alcarria – El Camino del Cid
description: Cycle the Camino del Cid: Álvar Fáñez Branch Trail, a 44.4-mile historical route through Guadalajara, Spain, tracing El Cid's loyal vassal.
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Almadrones traverse a landscape characterized by river valleys, natural parks, and historical pathways. The region features varied terrain, including sections along the Barranco del Río Dulce Natural Park and routes connecting to the historic Camino del Cid. Elevation gains are present, particularly on longer routes, indicating a mix of flat and undulating sections suitable for touring cyclists. Reservoirs and rural areas also define the cycling experience in this part of Spain. Best touring cycling routes around Almadrones The most popular touring cycling route is Pelegrina y Barranco del Río Dulce desde Aragosa — circular por el Parque Natural del Barranco del Río Dulce, a 22.6 miles (36 km) trail that takes 2 hours 45 minutes to complete. This route offers a circular journey through a natural park with river gorge views. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Camino del Cid: Álvar Fáñez Branch Trail, a difficult 44.3 miles (71 km) path. This trail follows a historic route, offering a long-distance cycling experience through diverse rural scenery. Local touring cyclists also love the Sigüenza – Sigüenza Cathedral loop from Mandayona, a 33.4 miles (54 km) trail leading through historical towns and countryside, often completed in about 4 hours 38 minutes. Touring cycling around Almadrones is defined by river valleys, natural parks, and historical routes, with options for challenging long-distance rides. The network offers options for different ability levels, though many routes in the area are considered difficult. The routes in Almadrones are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.0 stars from over 1 review. More than 40 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Almadrones's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What is the general difficulty level of touring cycling routes around Almadrones?
A: Touring cycling routes in the Almadrones area are generally considered challenging. All five featured routes are rated as difficult, indicating they are best suited for experienced cyclists looking for longer distances and varied terrain.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in Almadrones?
A: Yes, several routes offer a circular experience. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1187092789/pelegrina-y-barranco-del-rio-dulce-desde-aragosa-circular-por-el-parque-natural-del-barranco-del-rio-dulce" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pelegrina y Barranco del Río Dulce desde Aragosa&lt;/a&gt; route provides a circular journey through a natural park with river gorge views. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31504165" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sigüenza – Sigüenza Cathedral loop from Mandayona&lt;/a&gt;, which takes you through historical towns and countryside.

Q: What kind of scenery can I expect on touring bike trails near Almadrones?
A: The region offers diverse scenery, characterized by river valleys, natural parks like the Barranco del Río Dulce, and historical pathways. You'll encounter rural landscapes, reservoirs, and sections that follow ancient routes such as the Camino del Cid. Expect a mix of undulating terrain with elevation gains.

Q: Are there any routes that pass by historical sites or towns?
A: Absolutely. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1185291519/camino-del-cid-alvar-fanez-branch-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Camino del Cid: Álvar Fáñez Branch Trail&lt;/a&gt; follows a significant historic route. Additionally, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31504165" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sigüenza – Sigüenza Cathedral loop from Mandayona&lt;/a&gt; leads through historical towns and picturesque countryside, offering a glimpse into the area's heritage.

Q: What are some notable natural attractions or landmarks to see while cycling?
A: While cycling, you can explore the Barranco del Río Dulce Natural Park, known for its river gorge views. Nearby attractions include the fascinating &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5443557" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cívica Rock Dwellings and Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;, the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5443583" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Rocks of the Friars&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5459378" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cívica Rock-Cut Structures&lt;/a&gt;. Some routes also pass by reservoirs, such as the Cañamares River Reservoir and Pálmaces Reservoir.

Q: What is the typical length and duration of touring cycling routes in this area?
A: The touring cycling routes around Almadrones vary in length. You can find routes ranging from approximately 15 miles (24 km), like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31503155" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pelegrina Castle – Barranco del Río Dulce loop from Aragosa&lt;/a&gt;, up to 44 miles (71 km) for longer excursions such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1185291519/camino-del-cid-alvar-fanez-branch-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Camino del Cid: Álvar Fáñez Branch Trail&lt;/a&gt;. Durations can range from under 3 hours to over 5 hours, depending on the route and your pace.

Q: Are there any routes that feature reservoirs?
A: Yes, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20142442" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cañamares River Reservoir – Pálmaces Reservoir loop from Matillas&lt;/a&gt; is a notable route that takes you past these scenic water bodies, offering a different kind of landscape for your touring cycling experience.

Q: What do other touring cyclists say about the routes in Almadrones?
A: The touring cycling routes in Almadrones are highly regarded by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.0 stars. Cyclists often appreciate the varied terrain, the opportunity to explore natural parks, and the historical significance of some pathways.

Q: Are there any specific starting points for these touring cycling routes?
A: Routes often start from villages such as Aragosa, Mandayona, and Matillas. These locations serve as convenient access points for exploring the surrounding natural parks, historical trails, and reservoir areas.

Q: What kind of terrain should I expect on these touring cycling routes?
A: The terrain is varied, featuring a mix of flat sections and significant undulating parts with elevation gains. You'll cycle through river valleys, rural areas, and potentially some more rugged sections within natural parks. Given the 'difficult' rating for all routes, be prepared for challenging climbs and descents.

Q: Can I find routes that explore the Barranco del Río Dulce Natural Park?
A: Yes, the Barranco del Río Dulce Natural Park is a prominent feature of the region's cycling landscape. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1187092789/pelegrina-y-barranco-del-rio-dulce-desde-aragosa-circular-por-el-parque-natural-del-barranco-del-rio-dulce" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pelegrina y Barranco del Río Dulce desde Aragosa&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31503155" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pelegrina Castle – Barranco del Río Dulce loop from Aragosa&lt;/a&gt; offer excellent opportunities to explore this natural area and its river gorge views.
tips: Along the winding road that borders the valley of the Badiel River you reach the town of Ledanca, located on the Camino del Cid. The church of Our Lady of the Assumption, built on a mound, dominates the landscape of the town and the surrounding hills.

On the GU-109 road you will come across the Monastery of Valfermoso. Square-shaped monastery that surrounds an interior patio. It is a private property, but you can rest outside in the shade of the trees.

On the plateau of the Iberian system, the town of Trijueque was an outpost on the route to Torija marked today by the ruins of a castle, of which only parts of a tower remain. You must go up to the Alcarria viewpoint to get incredible views of the unique landscape of the area.

The first town on the cycle tour is Yela, which is hidden in a small valley between two alcarrias but was not spared from being razed during the Civil War, like other towns in the region. In the square, the portico of the church stands out, built in the fifties of the last century with ashlars from the Sigüenza quarries on the main facade of the sanctuary of Romanesque origin.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The Camino del Cid: Álvar Fáñez Branch Trail is designed to be completed as a single-day cycling trip. For touring cyclists, the route generally takes around 5 hours to complete, depending on your pace and stops.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the trail?
faqs.answer: The route primarily traverses relatively easy terrain, winding through meadows, scrubland, and cereal crops. You'll experience the Alcarria landscape with cultivated moorlands and hills. The surfaces are a mix, with approximately 42% tarmac and 57% unpaved tracks and trails. Some sections may involve minor roads with little traffic. While generally easy, some unpaved sections might have irregular surfaces or short, steep climbs.

faqs.question: Is this trail suitable for beginner cyclists?
faqs.answer: Yes, for general touring cycling on roads, the Álvar Fáñez Branch Trail is rated as having a low difficulty. The gravel bike version is also considered low difficulty. While the MTB version is low-medium, the route is generally manageable for a single day, making it suitable for cyclists with some experience, but not necessarily advanced skills.

faqs.question: What are the best times of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The recommended cycling season for the Álvar Fáñez Branch Trail is spring and, especially, autumn. These seasons offer pleasant temperatures, allowing you to avoid the cold winters and the hot, dry summers of the region.

faqs.question: What interesting sights can I see along the way?
faqs.answer: The trail offers picturesque rural scenery and passes through small, historic towns. Notable sights include modest examples of rural Romanesque architecture in towns like Villaseca de Henares, Utande, and Muduex. You can also visit the Church of Our Lady of the Assumption of Ledanca, the Monastery of Valfermoso de las Monjas, and enjoy the View from Mirador de la Alcarria. The walled town of Hita, with its imposing castle and literary connections, is a significant highlight.

faqs.question: Is the Camino del Cid: Álvar Fáñez Branch Trail a loop?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route is designed as a circular trail, starting and ending near Ledanca, allowing for a convenient day trip through the La Alcarria region.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the trailhead?
faqs.answer: As a circular route, you can typically start and park in one of the towns along the trail, such as Ledanca or Trijueque, which are mentioned in the route description. These rural towns usually offer street parking options suitable for accessing the trail.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: While the trail passes through rural areas, there are no specific restrictions mentioned regarding dogs. It is generally advisable to keep dogs on a leash, especially when passing through villages, cultivated areas, or near livestock, to ensure their safety and respect local regulations.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay entrance fees for the trail?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle the Álvar Fáñez Branch Trail itself. However, as part of the larger Camino del Cid network, you can obtain a 'Letter of safe conduct' which offers discounts at participating accommodations and establishments along the route.

faqs.question: Does this trail connect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route intersects with sections of other official trails. You will find parts of the Ramal de Álvar Fáñez - cicloturismo, Ruta de la lavanda, Cañada Real Galiana, Cañada Real de Merinas, and Camino de los Olmos along the way.

faqs.question: What makes this particular branch of the Camino del Cid unique?
faqs.answer: This branch is notable for its direct connection to the epic legend of El Cid. It commemorates the historical raid of Álvar Fáñez, a loyal vassal of El Cid, who plundered the Henares river basin to gather provisions. This historical narrative adds a unique cultural and literary dimension to the cycling experience, offering a journey through a historically significant, yet quiet, rural environment.
